beginner_level.name: Newton's First Law of Motion
beginner_level.description: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by an external force.
beginner_level.explanation: This law describes inertia - the resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion. Without external forces, objects maintain their current state.
examples: A book on a table remains stationary
A moving car continues moving if no brakes are applied
Passengers lurch forward when a car suddenly stops

beginner_level.name: Newton's Second Law of Motion
beginner_level.description: The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass.
beginner_level.explanation: Force equals mass times acceleration. A larger force produces greater acceleration, while larger mass requires more force for the same acceleration.
examples: Pushing a shopping cart: harder push = faster acceleration
A golf ball accelerates faster than a bowling ball with same force
Rockets accelerate slowly when full of fuel, faster when fuel depletes

beginner_level.name: Newton's Third Law of Motion
beginner_level.description: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.
beginner_level.explanation: Forces always come in pairs. When one object exerts force on another, the second object exerts an equal force back in the opposite direction.
examples: A swimmer pushing water backward propels forward
A rocket expels gas downward to move upward
Walking: feet push backward on ground, ground pushes forward on feet

beginner_level.name: Law of Universal Gravitation
beginner_level.description: Every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them.
beginner_level.explanation: Gravity is a fundamental force that attracts all objects with mass. The force weakens with distance (inverse square law).
examples: Earth pulls you downward with 9.8 m/s²
Moon orbits Earth due to gravitational attraction
Objects fall at the same rate (ignoring air resistance)

beginner_level.name: Law of Conservation of Energy
beginner_level.description: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another.
beginner_level.explanation: Total energy in an isolated system remains constant. Energy transforms between kinetic (motion) and potential (stored) forms.
examples: Swinging pendulum: PE at top converts to KE at bottom
Falling ball: PE converts to KE
Roller coaster: energy exchanges between KE and PE

beginner_level.name: Hooke's Law
beginner_level.description: The force exerted by a spring is directly proportional to the displacement from its equilibrium position.
beginner_level.explanation: Springs resist deformation with a restoring force proportional to how much they're stretched or compressed.

beginner_level.name: Ohm's Law
beginner_level.description: The voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it.
beginner_level.explanation: Voltage (V) equals current (I) multiplied by resistance (R). This fundamental law governs electrical circuits.
examples: Heating element gets hotter with more current
Light bulb resistance determines power usage

beginner_level.name: Snell's Law of Refraction
beginner_level.description: Light bends when passing between materials with different refractive indices.
beginner_level.explanation: The angle of refraction depends on the ratio of refractive indices and the angle of incidence.

intermediate_level.name: Newton's Law of Cooling
intermediate_level.description: The rate of cooling of an object is proportional to the difference between its temperature and the ambient temperature.
intermediate_level.explanation: Hot objects cool down faster when the temperature difference is larger. Cooling rate decreases as the object approaches ambient temperature.
examples: Hot coffee cooling in a cup
Forged metal cooling after blacksmith work

intermediate_level.name: Boyle's Law
intermediate_level.description: The pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume when temperature is constant.
intermediate_level.explanation: As a gas is compressed, its pressure increases proportionally. As it expands, pressure decreases.
examples: Bicycle pump getting harder to push

intermediate_level.name: Charles's Law
intermediate_level.description: The volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature when pressure is constant.
intermediate_level.explanation: Heating a gas causes it to expand; cooling causes it to contract.
examples: Air in a heated room rises
Helium balloons shrink in cold weather

intermediate_level.name: Ampere's Law
intermediate_level.description: The magnetic field around an electric current is proportional to the current flowing through a closed loop.
intermediate_level.explanation: Electric currents generate magnetic fields. The stronger the current, the stronger the magnetic field.

intermediate_level.name: Faraday's Law of Induction
intermediate_level.description: A changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an electromotive force (EMF) in the loop.
intermediate_level.explanation: Moving magnets or changing magnetic fields generate electric currents. This is the basis of electromagnetic induction.

intermediate_level.name: Young's Double Slit Experiment
intermediate_level.description: Light passing through two slits creates an interference pattern, demonstrating wave-particle duality.
intermediate_level.explanation: Light behaves as both a wave (creating interference) and a particle. This experiment is fundamental to quantum mechanics.

intermediate_level.name: Doppler Effect
intermediate_level.description: The frequency of a wave changes when the source or observer is moving relative to each other.
intermediate_level.explanation: Sound/light appears higher pitched when approaching and lower pitched when receding.

intermediate_level.name: Bernoulli's Principle
intermediate_level.description: In flowing fluids, pressure decreases as velocity increases.
intermediate_level.explanation: Fast-moving fluids have lower pressure than slow-moving ones. This explains lift generation.

advanced_level.name: Einstein's Theory of Special Relativity
advanced_level.description: The laws of physics are invariant in all inertial reference frames, and the speed of light is constant in all frames.
advanced_level.explanation: Time dilates, length contracts, and mass increases at high velocities. Energy and mass are interconvertible.

advanced_level.name: Einstein's Theory of General Relativity
advanced_level.description: Gravity is not a force but a curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy.
advanced_level.explanation: Massive objects warp spacetime around them. Objects follow geodesics (shortest paths) in curved spacetime.

advanced_level.name: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
advanced_level.description: You cannot simultaneously know both the exact position and exact momentum of a particle with arbitrary precision.
advanced_level.explanation: There is a fundamental limit to how precisely certain pairs of properties can be known. The more precisely you know position, the less you know momentum.

advanced_level.name: Schrödinger's Equation
advanced_level.description: The fundamental equation of quantum mechanics describing how the quantum state of a physical system changes over time.
advanced_level.explanation: This equation determines the wave function ψ which contains all information about a quantum system. Solving it reveals energy levels and particle behavior.

advanced_level.name: Maxwell's Equations
advanced_level.description: Four fundamental equations describing how electric and magnetic fields behave and interact.
advanced_level.explanation: These equations unify electricity, magnetism, and light. They predict electromagnetic waves traveling at the speed of light.

advanced_level.name: Planck's Equation (Energy Quantization)
advanced_level.description: Energy of a photon is proportional to its frequency. Energy comes in discrete packets called quanta.
advanced_level.explanation: Light consists of photons with energy determined by frequency. This explains the photoelectric effect and atomic spectra.

advanced_level.name: De Broglie Wavelength
advanced_level.description: Particles have associated wavelengths inversely proportional to their momentum.
advanced_level.explanation: All matter has wave properties. Heavier or faster particles have shorter wavelengths, while light particles have longer wavelengths.

advanced_level.name: Grand Unified Theory (GUT)
advanced_level.description: A theoretical framework attempting to unify electromagnetic, weak, and strong nuclear forces.
advanced_level.explanation: At extremely high energies, the three forces merge into a single unified force. String theory extends this further.

advanced_level.name: Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) Radiation
advanced_level.description: The CMB is remnant thermal radiation from the early universe, approximately 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
advanced_level.explanation: This leftover heat from the Big Bang fills all of space. Its uniform temperature provides strong evidence for the Big Bang theory.

advanced_level.name: String Theory
advanced_level.description: Fundamental particles are not points but tiny vibrating strings in a higher-dimensional space.
advanced_level.explanation: Different vibrational modes of strings correspond to different particles. String theory potentially unifies quantum mechanics and general relativity.
